speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred Gottschalk. Let me go back and pick up some details, and then we'll move on with the story. The-- the synagogue was tarred. This was people in the town who participated in this activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really don't know who did it. I have to presume there was a Nazi party in Oberwesel. And that while I was not aware of many Nazis in Oberwesel, Nazis being people in uniform, there were Nazi sympathizers all over town. Because there was a great deal to be gotten from the confiscation of Jewish property. For example, my grandfather was compelled to sell our home. And what happened was he sold the home to a Mr. Knopf, and for a ridiculous price of money. The money was, I think it was 12,000 marks, for a home plus a garden. The money was then deposited in the local bank, which was then confiscated. So you could not use the money that--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the sale--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you had so-- so ostensibly received from the sale-- from the sale of the house. But it was deposited in the bank, and then it was closed. The account was blocked is the word, [NON-ENGLISH] was blocked. And the systematization-- the systematic impoverishment that took place from '36 on, by limiting trade, by-- one of the reasons my father had to illegally go to the back country to trade with farmers is because he was not allowed to do business. So if you can't do business, and you can't earn income, and if you couldn't emigrate in a timely way, which was the pressure to build up then to get you out, you starve to death. And ultimately, that's what happened to the Jews who were unable to emigrate, like my grandfather and my aunts. They were herded together in a Judenhaus. They had to vacate the residences where they were, those that had not been deported, those that had not emigrated. And then eventually they were removed and went to Theresienstadt and Auschwitz, and other concentration camps subsequently. But the systematic policy of deprivation of income, harassment, physical punishment through beating, all kinds of illegal and horrible ways to deal with people, were visited upon the Jewish community, which had lived for hundreds of years in this town, and was visited upon them by their neighbors, some neighbors. Other neighbors, I know when I went back, we heard stories of people who risked their reputation or lives for bringing food to their Jewish neighbors. In one instance, a hole was battered through the basement walls. And neighbors brought food that were-- was delivered to this Judenhaus, so they didn't starve completely. On the other hand, they couldn't give them too much food. Because then it would become obvious that food was being made available to them. And then there was, of course, women were not permitted to keep any jewelry, except their wedding rings. So I remember my-- my mother giving up all of her gold jewelry, my grandmother's jewelry, my aunt's, the silverware, and it was put into a pillow case, which I dragged down to the district office of the police station. And for which they, in their fastidious way, gave me a receipt, which of course was totally worthless. They had no intention of ever returning any of this. But the receipt, they said, was very important. You might need it one day. You needed it to get out of the country. If you did get a visa to a foreign country, and you got an exit visa from Germany, then you had to show that you had complied with the law by having surrendered all your worldly goods. My mother and I, each person, was allowed to take 10 marks with them out of the country. That was for the voyage from Germany to America. So that was, in effect, the one way. On the other hand, they'll let you take out personal possessions with emigration, providing they were worthless, such as your furniture, books, Jewish books. They were worthless. They wanted them out of the country. And you were allowed to take two huge crates with you. That was the quota, one for each person emigrating. And the-- the subsequent difficulties that occurred after Kristallnacht made life hell for people.

speaker: interviewer
text: For instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, beatings, harassment, difficulties in going to a store. Jews were not permitted to go to most stores. Most stores would not sell their products or produce to Jews. So if you can't even go to buy food, or things that you need, how are you going to survive? Well, that was the whole point. You don't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you ate during that period and how you got the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- well, by the time we left in '39, before the war it was not-- it was bad. But it wasn't impossible. After the war started, it became impossible. That was the most difficult portion for my relatives. From '39 to '41 they literally lived from hand to mouth. Many of the men were put in concentration camps. Most of them never came home. My uncle Julius, who was arrested, [SIGHS] my-- my mother's youngest sister Alice, married Julius Katzenstein. And he was taken to Dachau. And I-- one day, I said to him, tell me something about this. And he said, no. I'm not going to tell you anything. Next morning, he said I'll tell you one thing. There was a roll call one morning, and by sheer accident, the two guys who were standing next to me-- one on the left, one on the right-- were just a few seconds late-- not minutes-- seconds late. He says, both of them were beaten to death on the spot for this infringement. I walked away. Now that's how terror works also. It's haphazard. It's on the one hand, there's premeditation and predictability. On the other hand, terror strikes, and that was also very calculated as part of the policy of terror. That was all over place all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from really November '38 until the time you left the country, your childhood ceased.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for a while, there was a Hebrew teacher who came in, Mr. Zelligman, and he stopped coming. And there was a time when the nuns could no longer offer me schooling either. Because they were under tremendous pressure. So by the time we left, and I found in our-- my passport-- we left Germany via Hamburg on June 13, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the journey from Oberwesel to Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on a train. And we were quite happy, my mother and I. But at the very last moment, as we were going on the gangplank, see the-- if you got halfway up to the gangplank plus one step you were, so to speak, under the protection of the ship, which was the SS Manhattan.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was an American ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: American ship. But they literally pulled her off the gangplank, and gave her a body search, to make sure that she was not carrying any valuables out of the country. Now as absurdities go, my grandfather, on his bar mitzvah, had received a gold dollar from a relative in America. And they sewed that into my lederhosen strap. Now if I had to be searched and they found that, I wouldn't be talking to you either, so the absurdity of the risks that you take. On the other hand, my grandfather said, when they get to America, you know, you've got to eat. So here at least is the gold dollar. I have the gold dollar. I look at it regularly to compel myself to remember these fragilities of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went by train. And did you have to wait in Hamburg for any length of time before disembarking-- or embarking? I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was-- it took-- it took a better part of a day to go through all of the-- the various processes. You know, we call them today, security checks. There they wanted to make sure that you were leaving penuriously, and that you had your ticket and your-- your entrance visa. At that point, they didn't want Jews to get back to Germany. And I received a special visa, because the quotas had been used up for children. But Roosevelt had a special children's quota, on top of what was then permitted. And I believe I came in under a special visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is why your visa came later than your mother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my mother's visa came in the beginning of April, and mine came in May. Again, it was a way of harassing, till the very end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And breaking up families and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once on the ship, what do you remember of the ship, and the feeling of-- as you say, being that halfway up the gangplank and one step?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a couple of days out, I remember there was another little boy on board whom I subsequently met as a young man. And both of us were seasick for the seven days it took to get from Hamburg to New York. It was-- for some reason, either the water was very rough, but I remember being in my bunk all the time. And my mother seemed to be immune to it, because she was on deck most of the time, bringing me apples.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in New York already. He was going to meet you there then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he left Holland, made it to America. And he was the only-- well, he and another member of the family met us at the dockside when we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- what was your memory of the day of arrival, as seasick as you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by then I was OK. The last couple of days, I think, were OK. I remember being on deck very early in the morning, very early in the morning, as the ship pulled into-- Ober-- to, funny Oberwesel-- the ship pulled into the harbor in New York. And I-- I caught glimpse of my father. And that was-- that was wonderful. That was terribly exciting.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was down on the-- on the key.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the dock, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he changed considerably?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not really, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had he done in Amsterdam? You said he was there eight months. Do you know what he was doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They were in the-- in like a DP camp, a detention camp. The Dutch had these holding centers. To their credit, they refused to send him back. Because the Germans had demanded his return, and they didn't return anybody, until they themselves were invaded. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he was-- he was at this-- I guess, the equivalent of a DP camp throughout that period of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long had he been in the United States before you and your mother arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- you know, it was end of the Depression. And the only work that was available to the greenhorns, which is the immigrants were called, was in sweatshops. So he had a job in a recycling factory, clothing recycling factory. And I remember he earned a huge salary of $14 a week. And when my mother began working, it was $7 a week. And I remember our first apartment, rented for $28 a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know address or the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. It was on-- the first apartment we lived on 30th Street and Fourth Avenue-- and the second apartment we lived on the-- 29th street and Fourth Avenue. And the second apartment was on 30th Street and Fifth Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: It went for $28 a month. What kind of an apartment was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a three-room apartment. I slept on a couch in the living room. And then when relatives came, progressively some got out. And my mother, despite our meager means, opened our home to whomever came-- and whoever came-- and I slept in the bathtub for a while, you know, to make room for people. I don't know how she did it. But there was always food on the table. And there were people who helped each other, you know. Those who were in the country a little longer made do with less, and helped those coming over, the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What about American organizations, immigrant organizations, were they of any help?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't exist, as far as I know. They weren't there. I mean, people who were there were relatives, and others who wanted to help. But, you know, you can't think of American organizational life then in today's terms. They were minuscule, relatively speaking, and they were swamped.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish organizations, and the immigrant organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, yeah, with all these refugees coming in, those who were able to come in. And they had very meager resources, I think. But there were exceptions, I'm sure. But people who went to Manhattan had an easier time than those who went to Brooklyn or Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the agencies were in Manhattan. You know, and-- but they were New York oriented, rather than suburb oriented.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why-- you were in Brooklyn, because that was basically where your father had found work, and therefore was living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's where relatives offered him a place to live. And then after he got work, he rented an apartment for us. And so we began our life in America, in Brooklyn, in a very heavy Italian neighborhood on one side, Irish and Polish on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fascinating. The fact that you were not only Jewish, but in the eyes of the Americans, primarily German, did that make your life--

speaker: interviewee
text: Miserable, absolutely miserable. [LAUGHING] For a long-- for quite a while, we-- I had no clothes. So I used to wear lederhosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which are those leather shorts, with the halter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I would come home black and blue most of-- most days of the week. Because these kids looked upon me as some sort of a-- a greenie, again the word greenhorn. And it wasn't until I became a street fighter myself that I was accepted into a gang. And well, we did that to other people. You know, that's the-- [LAUGHTER] that's the way these things operate. I also learned how to run very fast. The Italian neighborhood was the most passive and friendly. The Irish and Polish neighborhoods, you had to move through very quickly. And since a nickel was what it cost to take the trolley car to Hebrew school, I would often pocket the nickel, and run the 33 blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so began your sports career. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sports-- my sports career began.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it-- what was the process of becoming an American like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was brought home one day by a streetcar conductor, who stopped, because our house was-- our apartment house was a half a block from where the street car stopped. And he brought me in, and he said, next time your son does this, I'm going to have him arrested. I was riding on back of this the trolley car to save the nickel. So that's how-- then I went to work in America myself, selling newspapers and shining shoes on the weekends at the subway station.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you charge to shine a pair of shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the shine, the price of the shine was a nickel. Your tip was usually a dime. My uncles gave me a quarter. And that was a fortune. I could go to the movies on a Sunday for $0.15, and sit all day, which is what I did. I once told Ronald Reagan, when he was president, that he taught me English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because he was-- he was in all these cowboy movies at the time. [LAUGHS] And that's how I learned-- I learned English through my-- through the ear, largely by attending lots and lots of movies. They also filled my fantasy life as a kid, see?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start school in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately almost, in the fall of '39. I went to PS 172. I had wonderful teachers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only refugee child there, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as far as I can remember. But we had the tradition of English teachers, British teachers, in the public schools. And they taught me great respect for the English sentence. And the-- the little I know of the English language they taught me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as the language became easier, did life become easier-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, language is the key. My father learned English, because he learned-- he was a quick study. He was a very fine mathematician. I think he probably, if he had his druthers, that's what he would have wanted to be. My mother never really mastered it, and she had neologisms that were wonderful. One day we're walking down Fifth Avenue, and she sees a very attractive man all spatted up and everything. And she says to me, Alfred, look at that man. Doesn't he look extinguished?

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So she would come up with these malapropisms. They were funny. But she had a great eye, and she could spell-- smell a phony across the room faster than anybody I've ever met. So they were intuitive gifts. My parents were very different. One was very rational. The other was very emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think back on Oberwesel at all, or would-- did it become a receding memory or was it still very lively in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is unusual is that we brought these hundreds of family photographs with us, postcards, and settings of the family in Oberwesel. And we would go through these regularly. We'd say this is aunt so-and-so, uncle so-and-so, and to this day, these people have been dead many, many years. I still know who they are, because my mother and father would go through all the photographs and identified the people. Which I'm going to do with my grandchildren. Because they should at least know. So that's how we-- we became reacquainted with Oberwesel, and with the context. I didn't really want to go back, I had no-- no intention of going back, but I did go back twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever tell, during this period of time, Americans what you had experienced in Oberwesel, what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You didn't talk-- there were-- who were the Americans? Your neighbors were Polish, Italian, Irish, who had their own problems. And it was a time of immigration restriction. Immigrants were looked upon as competitors for jobs. So there was no really camaraderie, even in the workplace. The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] ability to communicate about what happened was very limited. No one really cared, except your own family group. And we each knew each other's stories by heart, 50 times. So you get to know what happened, to whom, when, and where. But outsiders were not really interested, including the larger American Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did a paper for the Leo Baeck Institute on the 50th anniversary of Kristallnacht, and did a research for it on the attitudes of Jewish organizations to Kristallnacht. And it was a shocking indictment of the apathy of most American Jewish organizations who wanted to deny the full-blown evil that existed there. Because it would have forced them to do something about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't talk easily about it. I know many survivors whom I've met through the United States Holocaust Council, who didn't speak at all until just a few years ago. Then finally it dawned on them, if they don't tell their story to their children or their grandchildren, it's going to die with them. And then they very selectively began. Because there's an enormous shock element in most of these stories, horror stories, people who actually were in the camps. And the few, relatively speaking statistically, who survived have horrible stories to tell. So from-- from the very earliest times in the United States, this was something that talked about only in the family circles only, on certain occasions. And then outside the home, life went on pretty much without reference to what was happening in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you needed to survive economically. It was a country. The country was in-- in a major recession. It wasn't until America was way into the war that there was any kind of an economic uptick that was measurable.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- your parents decided then to take citizenship, and began preparing then to remain in the country. Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. There was no-- first place, there was never any thought of going back to Germany. My mother, although she was invited to go back, wouldn't give it more than a second's thought. My father, of course, died at a very early age in America. And they were interested in making a living and a livelihood here, and having a future here, especially for their child. So they had no intention of going anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to public grammar school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --then, and then went on to high school in Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Boys High School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were there, did you have particular studies or particular activities that you enjoyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I loved-- I loved school. And I joined the football team. Because someone told me that it wasn't enough to be smart to get a scholarship. But you had to be smart and an athlete. So I began playing football. And by the time I graduated Boys High, I was designated the outstanding boy high school senior that year, in 1946-7 in Brooklyn. And I had several scholarships. I couldn't take any of them. Because my father died in '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leukemia. And then suddenly my mother became very ill. And she had to stop working. And I had start doing something to earn some real money. So I became a semi-professional football player.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get into that, let me talk about-- or tell us about your Jewish life in the United States, your religious life during this period. When-- when you arrived, did you worship with other immigrants? Did you join an American congregation? How did you reestablish Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my dad did sort of the synagogue searching out. And he-- we started out, since he was a traditional Jew, to go to an orthodox synagogue. And he-- it turned him off completely. And then he found a conservative synagogue that he thought we should spend the first high holidays at. And I remember walking with him and my mother with great excitement to the synagogue, and getting to the door, and somebody said to him, where is your ticket or do you have a ticket?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father turned to my mother and said, what-- what do you mean, ticket? What is this, a theater? And finally somebody came forward and said, oh, another greenhorn. So they let us in, and they put us up into the pauper's gallery. And we went back the following day, sat up there again. And then suddenly an opportunity occurred. Because the cantor developed laryngitis. And he was unable to read the Torah. And the place was packed, of course, and the rabbi in a cold sweat asked, is there anybody here who can read the Torah? No one budged. So my father tried to dope out what was going on here. And he finally out of sheer amazement, said, no one here could do this? Well, the service stopped cold. So he got up, and he walked all the way down from the upper balcony up to the bimah, and addressed the rabbi and said in his best German, [SPEAKING GERMAN] Rabbi, may I read?

speaker: interviewee
text: And because most of the men in Oberwesel could read the Torah, if not at sight, then with practice. But my dad knew that portion, and he sang it beautifully. And from that point on, our Americanization took place in that synagogue, which became the place where I developed a love of Judaism. As the rabbi-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this incident, I guess, was-- it was September of 1939. And that rabbi died in 1940 or so, and then a new rabbi came in, who made this a very exciting place. Because he had just left his practice as a lawyer and decided to be a rabbi. And gave us kids, boys and girls, a wonderful opportunity to speak publicly, and to help in the war effort. We sold war bonds by giving speeches every Shabbos morning. And then they'd send us out to the [NON-ENGLISH] on the east side. And I had a Yiddish speech prepared, and gave, and tried to sell war bonds. So the synagogue also was a place where I learned Yiddish, from this Cantor Matlans, a wonderful man. He was the original Mr. Five by Five. Between him and Rabbi Silverstein, the place was jumping all the time. And they were-- they were human. If you come to services Shabbos morning, they gave you tickets to movies in the afternoon, you know. If you didn't come to services Shabbos morning, you got no tickets for the movies in the afternoon. Sundays were Sunday school, and then weekdays Hebrew school. And it was just a home, really a home for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the congregation again?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called the 9th Street Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's stop here for a minute. We'll change tape.
